topics.title: Strength of Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL307 - Strength of Materials Laboratory
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Torsion Test on a Mild Steel Circular Shaft — Determination of Shear Modulus (Rigidity Modulus, G)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion Test:** A destructive mechanical test in which a cylindrical specimen (solid or hollow) is subjected to a gradually increasing **twisting moment (torque, T)** about its longitudinal axis until failure occurs. The test determines the **shear strength**, **modulus of rigidity (G)**, and **angle of twist (θ)** of the material — key design parameters for shafts, axles, springs, and helical components used in mechanical, civil, and aerospace engineering.

**Modulus of Rigidity (G):** Also called the **Shear Modulus**, it is the ratio of **shear stress (τ)** to **shear strain (γ)** within the elastic limit, expressed in **GPa (Gigapascals)** or **N/mm²**. For mild steel, the standard value is **G ≈ 79.3 GPa (≈ 80 GPa)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine holding a chalk piece between your two palms and twisting them in opposite directions. The chalk resists the twist — that internal resistance is **torque (T)**, and the tiny angular rotation you cause is the **angle of twist (θ)**. The *stiffness* of the chalk against twisting is essentially what **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** measures.

- A **thick** chalk = high resistance (large d → high G contribution).
- A **rubber** stick = twists easily (low G).
- A **steel** rod = resists twisting strongly (high G).

So the torsion test is simply: *apply known twists, measure how much the shaft resists, and back-calculate the material's intrinsic stiffness against shearing deformation.*

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Reference:** IS 1717 : 1985 (Reaffirmed 2005) — *Method for Simple Torsion Testing of Steel Wire and Strip.* For round bar specimens, **ASTM A938** is widely followed internationally.
>
> - **Universal Gas Constant / Geometric Constant for Solid Shaft:** $k = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}$ (units: m⁻³)
> - **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** for solid circular shaft: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
> - **Standard Specimen Gauge Length (L):** typically 50 mm to 100 mm
> - **Recommended Loading Rate:** uniform, quasi-static (no impact loading)
> - **Standard Mild Steel Diameter Range:** 10 mm to 25 mm

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theory of Pure Torsion — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Pure Torsion Assumption:** The shaft is subjected to *no axial, bending, or transverse loads* — only equal and opposite twisting moments at the two ends. This ensures a state of **pure shear** on any cross-section.
2. **Plane Sections Remain Plane & Radial:** Even after twisting, cross-sections perpendicular to the axis remain plane, and *radii remain straight lines* (Saint-Venant's principle for circular sections).
3. **Deformation Geometry:** A longitudinal line on the surface, originally parallel to the axis, tilts by an angle $\gamma$ called the **shear strain at the surface**.
4. **Stress Distribution:** Because strain varies linearly from the centre (zero) to the outer surface (maximum), and the material obeys Hooke's Law in shear ($\tau = G\gamma$), the **shear stress $\tau$** is also **zero at the centre** and **maximum at the outer surface** ($r = d/2$).
5. **Equilibrium Condition:** The internal resisting torque (integral of $\tau \cdot r$ over the cross-section) must equal the externally applied torque $T$. This yields the celebrated **Torsion Equation**.
6. **Boundary Conditions:** At the free surface ($r = d/2$), $\tau = \tau_{\max}$. At the shaft centre ($r = 0$), $\tau = 0$. At the fixed end, the angle of twist is zero (reference datum).

### 2.2 The Torsion Equation (Derivable, High-Yield)

$$
T = \frac{\tau_{\max} \cdot J}{r} = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}
$$

The three equivalent forms, all examined in KTU 2024 ESE papers:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = Applied Torque (**N·m**)
- $J$ = Polar Moment of Inertia of the cross-section (**m⁴** or **mm⁴**)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ (**N/m²** or **N/mm²**)
- $r$ = Radial distance from the centre (**m** or **mm**)
- $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity (**N/m²** or **N/mm²**)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist over length $L$ (**radians**)
- $L$ = Gauge length over which twist is measured (**m** or **mm**)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorise This Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Polar Moment of Inertia (solid) | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | m⁴, mm⁴ | Geometric property of solid shaft |
| 2 | Polar Moment of Inertia (hollow) | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | m⁴, mm⁴ | Hollow shaft design |
| 3 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3}$ | N/m², N/mm² | Solid shaft; occurs at surface |
| 4 | Shear Strain at Surface | $\gamma = \dfrac{r \, \theta}{L} = \dfrac{d \, \theta}{2L}$ | radians (rad) | Geometry of twist |
| 5 | Angle of Twist | $\theta = \dfrac{T \, L}{G \, J}$ | radians | Total angular deformation |
| 6 | Modulus of Rigidity | $G = \dfrac{T \, L}{J \, \theta} = \dfrac{8 \, T \, L}{\pi d^4 \, \theta}$ | N/mm², GPa | **The key experimental output** |
| 7 | Torque from lever-arm load | $T = W \times a$ | N·m | Test rig: weight × arm length |
| 8 | Angle from dial gauge | $\theta = \dfrac{\phi_{\text{reading}}}{a} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$ | rad | Convert dial reading (mm) using arm *a* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** Always express $\theta$ in **radians**, length in **metres (or mm consistently)**, and torque in **N·m (or N·mm)** so that the unit of $G$ emerges correctly as **N/m² (Pa)**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The torsion test is **not academic** — it directly drives the design of:
- **Drive shafts** in automobiles (e.g., a Maruti 800 driveshaft transmits engine torque to the differential).
- **Propeller shafts** in ships and aircraft.
- **Drill pipes** in oil rigs — operating in deep, twisted, hostile environments.
- **Helical and torsion springs** in clutches and watches.
- **Structural members under seismic torsion** in multi-storey buildings.

The value of $G$ obtained from this experiment is then plugged into **FEA software (ANSYS, Abaqus)** to predict twist and stress in real components.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torsion Equation from First Principles

**Assumption 1 — Geometry of Deformation:** Consider a shaft of diameter $d$, length $L$, with a generator line $AB$ drawn on the surface. After applying torque $T$ at the free end, point $B$ moves to $B'$, the shaft twists by angle $\theta$, and the generator tilts by shear strain $\gamma$.

$$
\text{From the geometry of the twisted surface element:}
$$

$$
\tan \gamma = \frac{BB'}{AB} = \frac{r \, \theta}{L}
$$

For small strains ($\gamma$ in radians), $\tan \gamma \approx \gamma$, so:

$$
\gamma = \frac{r \, \theta}{L}
$$

**Assumption 2 — Hooke's Law in Shear:** Within the elastic limit, the shear stress is proportional to shear strain:

$$
\tau = G \gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \frac{G \, r \, \theta}{L}
$$

**Assumption 3 — Equilibrium:** The internal resisting torque on a cross-section equals the integral of the elemental torque $\tau \, dA \cdot r$ over the area:

$$
T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA = \int_A \left( \frac{G \, r \, \theta}{L} \right) r \, dA
$$

$$
T = \frac{G \theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA
$$

By definition, the polar moment of inertia is:

$$
J = \int_A r^2 \, dA
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\, T = \frac{G \, J \, \theta}{L} \,}
$$

Rearranging the three equivalent forms:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}
$$

This is the **Torsion Equation**, the heart of the experiment.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polar Moment of Inertia for a Solid Circular Shaft

Consider a thin concentric ring of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, on the cross-section. Its area is:

$$
dA = 2 \pi r \, dr
$$

Therefore:

$$
J = \int_0^{R} r^2 \cdot (2 \pi r) \, dr = 2 \pi \int_0^{R} r^3 \, dr
$$

$$
J = 2 \pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^{R} = \frac{2 \pi R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}
$$

Substituting $R = d/2$:

$$
J = \frac{\pi (d/2)^4}{2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Shear Stress Formula

From the torsion equation:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{\max}}{J}
$$

For a solid shaft, the maximum stress occurs at the outer fibre where $r_{\max} = d/2$:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, \tau_{\max} = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi d^3} \,}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Experimental Formula for Modulus of Rigidity

The test rig uses a lever arm of length $a$ (in metres) at the free end, with a hanging weight $W$ (in Newtons). The applied torque is:

$$
T = W \times a
$$

The angle of twist is measured by a dial gauge that reads a linear displacement $x$ at radius $a$:

$$
\phi_{\text{arm}} = \frac{x}{a} \quad \text{(radians of arm rotation)}
$$

Since the dial gauge is mounted on the arm of length $a$, the angle of twist of the shaft over length $L$ equals the arm rotation:

$$
\theta = \frac{x}{a}
$$

Plugging into the torsion equation solved for $G$:

$$
G = \frac{T \, L}{J \, \theta} = \frac{(W \cdot a) \cdot L}{\left( \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{x}{a} \right)}
$$

$$
G = \frac{32 \, W \, a^2 \, L}{\pi \, d^4 \, x}
$$

This is the **primary working formula** evaluated in the KTU lab record.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Model Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **type of numerical that appears in Part B (14 marks)** — practice it.

**Given:**
- Diameter of mild steel shaft, $d = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m
- Gauge length, $L = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- Lever arm length, $a = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m
- Applied load, $W = 50$ N
- Dial gauge reading, $x = 0.85$ mm $= 0.00085$ m

**Step 1 — Torque:**

$$
T = W \times a = 50 \times 0.150 = 7.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Polar Moment of Inertia:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.020)^4}{32} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Angle of Twist:**

$$
\theta = \frac{x}{a} = \frac{0.00085}{0.150} = 5.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Modulus of Rigidity:**

$$
G = \frac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot \theta} = \frac{7.5 \times 0.100}{1.5708 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.667 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
G = \frac{0.75}{8.901 \times 10^{-11}} = 8.426 \times 10^{9} \text{ N/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\, G \approx 84.26 \text{ GPa} \,}
$$

**Comparison:** The standard textbook value of $G$ for mild steel is **79.3 GPa**.
- Percentage error: $\dfrac{|84.26 - 79.3|}{79.3} \times 100 = 6.25\%$

This is **well within the ±10% tolerance** accepted by KTU lab examiners.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error & Remedies

| Source of Error | Effect on G | Mitigation |
|-----------------|-------------|------------|
| Misalignment of shaft axis | Premature bending, false twist | Use V-block alignment, spirit level |
| Friction in bearings | Over-estimate G | Lubricate bearings; subtract zero-load deflection |
| Non-uniform diameter | Concentrated stress | Use $\bar{d}$ averaged from 3 readings (end + middle) |
| Dial gauge zero error | Systematic shift | Re-zero before every load step |
| Plastic deformation in final readings | Non-linear response | Stay strictly within elastic limit |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Torsion Testing Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Dead Weights Pan] --> B[Pulley and Cable System]
    B --> C[Lever Arm radius a]
    C --> D[Specimen Holding Chuck - Fixed End]
    D --> E[Mild Steel Specimen]
    E --> F[Rotating Chuck - Free End]
    F --> G[Dial Gauge Mounted on Arm]
    G --> H[Dial Gauge Display x mm]
    E --> I[Base Frame - Rigid Bed]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    H --> J[Calculation Block: G = 32 W a^2 L over pi d^4 x]
    J --> K[Output: Modulus of Rigidity in GPa]
    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 State Diagram — Experimental Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Measure d, L, a] --> S2[Step 2: Mount specimen in chucks]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Set dial gauge to zero]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply small initial load to remove slack]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Re-zero dial gauge]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Add weight in increments of 5 to 10 N]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Record dial reading x at each load]
    S7 --> S8{Load still in elastic limit?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S6
    S8 -- No --> S9[Step 8: Plot T vs theta graph]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9: Calculate slope = GJ over L]
    S10 --> S11[Step 10: Compute G and report in GPa]
    style S11 fill:#577590,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Free-Body Concept — Torque Transmission Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Applied Load W N] --> P2[Torque T = W times a N m]
    P2 --> P3[Internal Shear Stress tau distribution]
    P3 --> P4[Zero at centre r = 0]
    P3 --> P5[Maximum tau_max at surface r = d/2]
    P5 --> P6[Angle of twist theta over length L]
    P6 --> P7[Modulus of Rigidity G computed]
    style P2 fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#e63946,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style P7 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**State the torsion equation and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The torsion equation for a circular shaft under pure torsion is:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \, \theta}{L}
$$

Where:
- $T$ — External applied torque (**N·m**).
- $J$ — Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section about its centroidal axis (**m⁴**).
- $\tau$ — Shear stress at a fibre located at radius $r$ (**N/m²**).
- $r$ — Radial distance of the fibre from the centre (**m**).
- $G$ — Modulus of rigidity of the material (**N/m²**).
- $\theta$ — Angle of twist over length $L$ (**radians**).
- $L$ — Length of the shaft over which the twist is measured (**m**).

*(½ mark per term × 6 terms = 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between modulus of elasticity (E) and modulus of rigidity (G).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Modulus of Elasticity (E) | Modulus of Rigidity (G) |
|----------|---------------------------|-------------------------|
| Stress type | Tensile / Compressive (Normal) | Shear |
| Strain type | Linear (longitudinal) | Angular (shear) |
| Formula | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ |
| Typical mild steel value | ≈ 200 GPa | ≈ 79.3 GPa |
| Related by | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | where $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Derive the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft subjected to a pure torque. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a torsion test on a mild steel solid shaft of diameter **22 mm** and gauge length **120 mm**, the following data were recorded. The lever arm length is **200 mm**. Plot the torque vs. angle-of-twist graph and determine the **modulus of rigidity** of the material.

| Load $W$ (N) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dial reading $x$ (mm) | 0 | 0.42 | 0.85 | 1.27 | 1.71 | 2.13 |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law**.
2. The cross-section is **circular and remains circular** after twisting.
3. Cross-sections **remain plane** and perpendicular to the axis (no warping).
4. Radial lines remain **straight and undistorted** after twisting.
5. The shaft is subjected to **pure torsion** (no bending or axial load).
6. The deformation is **uniform** along the length $L$.

**Derivation:**

Consider a generator $AB$ on the surface of a shaft of length $L$ and radius $R$. After applying torque $T$, the line tilts by shear strain $\gamma$.

$$
\gamma = \frac{BB'}{AB} = \frac{R \theta}{L}
$$

By Hooke's Law in shear: $\tau = G \gamma = \dfrac{G R \theta}{L}$

Equilibrium of an elemental ring of area $dA$ at radius $r$:

$$
T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA = \int_A \frac{G \, \theta}{L} \cdot r^2 \, dA = \frac{G \theta}{L} \cdot J
$$

Where $J = \int_A r^2 \, dA$ is the polar moment of inertia.

Rearranging and substituting $\tau \cdot r = G \, r^2 \theta / L$:

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L} \,}
$$

**Mark distribution:** Assumptions (2), Geometry of deformation (2), Hooke's law (1), Equilibrium integral (1.5), Final boxed equation (0.5) = **7 marks**.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Torque for each load:**
$T = W \times a = W \times 0.200$ m

| $W$ (N) | $T$ (N·m) |
|---------|-----------|
| 20 | 4.0 |
| 40 | 8.0 |
| 60 | 12.0 |
| 80 | 16.0 |
| 100 | 20.0 |

**Step 2 — Compute Angle of Twist for each dial reading:**
$\theta = \dfrac{x}{a} = \dfrac{x}{200 \text{ mm}}$

| $x$ (mm) | $\theta$ (rad) |
|----------|----------------|
| 0.42 | 0.00210 |
| 0.85 | 0.00425 |
| 1.27 | 0.00635 |
| 1.71 | 0.00855 |
| 2.13 | 0.01065 |

**Step 3 — Compute Polar Moment of Inertia:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.022)^4}{32} = 2.298 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 4 — Plot $T$ vs. $\theta$:** A straight line through origin is expected (elastic region). Slope $m = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta \theta}$.

Using the first and last points: $m = \dfrac{20 - 4}{0.01065 - 0.00210} = \dfrac{16}{0.00855} = 1871.3$ N·m/rad

**Step 5 — Compute G:**

From $T = \dfrac{G J \theta}{L}$, slope $m = \dfrac{G J}{L}$, hence:

$$
G = \frac{m \cdot L}{J} = \frac{1871.3 \times 0.120}{2.298 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
G = 9.77 \times 10^{9} \text{ N/m}^2 = 97.7 \text{ GPa}
$$

This is in reasonable agreement with the **standard value of 79.3 GPa** for mild steel (error ≈ 23%, indicating a possible need to repeat or check alignment; for KTU valuation, full marks are awarded if method is correct).

**Mark distribution:** Torque table (1), Angle table (1), J calculation (1), Graph plot (1.5), Slope determination (1), Final G (1.5) = **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** A solid circular shaft of diameter **40 mm** is required to transmit **30 kW** at **200 rpm**. Determine:
(i) the torque transmitted, and
(ii) the maximum shear stress induced in the shaft.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **shear stress distribution** across a circular cross-section under torsion, and state why a **hollow shaft is preferred** over a solid shaft of the same weight for power transmission.
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**(i) Torque transmitted:**

Power $P = 30$ kW $= 30{,}000$ W, Speed $N = 200$ rpm

$$
P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{P \times 60}{2 \pi N}
$$

$$
T = \frac{30{,}000 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 200} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{1256.64} = 1432.4 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Shear Stress:**

For a solid shaft of diameter $d = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 1432.4}{\pi \times (0.040)^3}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{22{,}918.4}{2.0106 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.14 \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\, \tau_{\max} \approx 114 \text{ MPa} \,}
$$

**Mark distribution:** Power formula (1.5), Torque calculation (2), $\tau_{\max}$ formula (1.5), Final value (2) = **7 marks**.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Shear Stress Distribution in a Solid Shaft:**

- At the **centre** ($r = 0$): $\tau = 0$
- At radius $r$: $\tau \propto r$ (linear variation)
- At the **outer surface** ($r = d/2$): $\tau = \tau_{\max}$ (maximum)

This gives a **linear distribution** — zero at centre, maximum at the periphery, in the form of a cone (in 3D) or a triangle (in 2D cross-section).

**Hollow Shaft vs. Solid Shaft — Why Hollow is Preferred:**

In a solid shaft, the **central core material carries negligible stress** (since $\tau$ at the centre is zero). Hence, this material is essentially "wasted" in terms of load-bearing capacity. Removing it to form a hollow shaft:

1. **Saves weight** without compromising strength (since the high-stress material at the outer fibres is retained).
2. **Increases $\tau_{\max}$** for the same outer diameter, or allows a **smaller diameter** for the same torque.
3. **Stiffer** for the same weight — the polar moment of inertia $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$ can be tailored by choosing the right $D/d$ ratio.
4. **Better material economy** — important in aerospace and automotive applications.
5. **Passages for fluids or cooling** can be routed through the hollow centre.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU's favourite question:** Hollow shaft with given $D/d$ ratio asking to compare strength-to-weight ratio with a solid shaft. Memorise the formula $J_{\text{hollow}} = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$ and the optimum $D/d \approx 0.6$ for maximum torque per unit weight.

**Mark distribution:** Stress distribution with diagram (3), Hollow-shaft advantages (4) = **7 marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $d$ in mm but $L$ in m — instant 1 mark cut. **Always standardise** to SI (m, N, Pa) OR to mm-units (mm, N, N/mm²).
> 2. **Forgetting radians:** Plugging $x$ in mm and $a$ in mm into $\theta = x/a$ yields a *dimensionless* number — good — but if you treat $\theta$ as degrees, the final $G$ will be off by a factor of 57.3. **Always confirm radians.**
> 3. **Confusing $J$ (polar) with $I$ (area moment):** They are different quantities. $J$ is for torsion; $I$ is for bending. KTU often tests this distinction.
> 4. **Not plotting the graph:** A $T$ vs. $\theta$ plot is **mandatory** in the lab record. Skipping it = 1.5 mark loss.
> 5. **No zero-correction:** Always record dial reading with no load and subtract from all readings to remove zero error.
> 6. **Exceeding elastic limit:** Loading the shaft into the plastic region invalidates Hooke's Law. Stop at the first deviation from linearity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Torsion equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$ — the master formula.
- [x] **Polar moment of inertia (solid):** $J = \pi d^4 / 32$.
- [x] **Polar moment of inertia (hollow):** $J = \pi (D^4 - d^4) / 32$.
- [x] **Max shear stress (solid):** $\tau_{\max} = 16 T / (\pi d^3)$.
- [x] **Angle of twist:** $\theta = TL / (GJ)$.
- [x] **Modulus of rigidity from test:** $G = 32 W a^2 L / (\pi d^4 x)$ (lever-arm rig).
- [x] **Experimental $G$ for mild steel ≈ 79.3 GPa** (accept 70–90 GPa).
- [x] **Stress is zero at the centre, maximum at the surface.**
- [x] **Saint-Venant's principle** applies to circular sections only.
- [x] **Pure torsion** requires only twisting moments — no axial or bending loads.
- [x] **Hooke's Law in shear:** $\tau = G\gamma$ is valid **only in the elastic region**.
- [x] **Standard mild steel:** E ≈ 200 GPa, G ≈ 79.3 GPa, ν ≈ 0.30.
- [x] **Relation:** $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ — useful for cross-checking.
- [x] **Power-torque-speed:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ (with $P$ in W, $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m).
- [x] **Hollow shaft preferred** for high torque-to-weight applications.
- [x] **Always standardise units before substitution** — golden rule of KTU lab valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
